title: GitLab CI/CD - Hands-On Lab: Working with CI/CD Components
headings: GitLab CI/CD - Hands-On Lab: Working with CI/CD Components
text:
GitLab CI/CD - Hands-On Lab: Working with CI/CD Components

Estimated time to complete: 15 minutes

Estimated time to complete: 15 minutes

As Tanuki Enterprises scaled from one project to dozens, your team discovered they were copying and pasting the same CI/CD configurations across every repository. The problems multiplied quickly:

Configuration Drift : Each project has slightly different versions of the same jobs, making it impossible to maintain consistency

Maintenance Nightmare : When a security update requires changing a configuration, teams must manually update dozens of .gitlab-ci.yml files

No Standardization : New projects reinvent the wheel, with each developer writing their own version of common tasks

Knowledge Silos : Best practices from one team don’t propagate to others because there’s no central way to share CI/CD patterns

Wasted Effort : Developers spend hours configuring pipelines instead of focusing on application code

In this lab, you’ll learn to create reusable CI/CD components that can be shared across projects and teams. You’ll build a component catalog that allows Tanuki Enterprises to standardize their pipeline configurations, maintain them centrally, and version them just like application code.

Task A. Creating a Component

Let’s create a component to use in our GitLab project.

Navigate to your My Test Group by clicking it in the breadcrumb at the top of the page.

Navigate to your My Test Group by clicking it in the breadcrumb at the top of the page.

From your My Test Group in GitLab, click the New project button.

Creating a Component

Let’s create a component to use in our GitLab project.

Navigate to your My Test Group by clicking it in the breadcrumb at the top of the page.

Navigate to your My Test Group by clicking it in the breadcrumb at the top of the page.

From your My Test Group in GitLab, click the New project button.

From your My Test Group in GitLab, click the New project button.

Click the Create blank project tile.

Click the Create blank project tile.

Name your project Tanuki App Component .

Name your project Tanuki App Component .

Leave all other values as their default, and click the Create project button and wait for GitLab to redirect you to the new project’s main page. Now, we need to set up the configuration for the component.

Leave all other values as their default, and click the Create project button and wait for GitLab to redirect you to the new project’s main page.

Now, we need to set up the configuration for the component.

In the repository of your Example Component project, click the + button, then click the New Directory option.

In the repository of your Example Component project, click the + button, then click the New Directory option.

For the directory name, type in templates . Make sure that it is in lower case.

For the directory name, type in templates . Make sure that it is in lower case.

Click the Commit changes button, and commit it to the main branch.

Click the Commit changes button, and commit it to the main branch.

Ensure you are now in the new templates directory. Click on the + button, then click the New file button.

r the directory name, type in templates . Make sure that it is in lower case.

Click the Commit changes button, and commit it to the main branch.

Click the Commit changes button, and commit it to the main branch.

Ensure you are now in the new templates directory. Click on the + button, then click the New file button.

Ensure you are now in the new templates directory. Click on the + button, then click the New file button.

Type in sample-template.yml as the file name.

Type in sample-template.yml as the file name.

In the body of the file, copy the following text to create your sample component: spec : inputs : stage : default : test --- component-job : script : echo "The Tanuki Component is live" stage : $[[ inputs.stage ]] Here, we are creating a component with an input called stage . The stage input has a default value of ’test’, which we will override in our other project.

In the body of the file, copy the following text to create your sample component:

Here, we are creating a component with an input called stage . The stage input has a default value of ’test’, which we will override in our other project.

Click Commit changes , and then click Commit changes in the pop-up screen. Now we have a component file, but in order for the component to be accessible by other projects, we need to publish the component. Note that since the project is private, it will only be accessible by you.

Click Commit changes , and then click Commit changes in the pop-up screen.

Task B. Publishing the Component

On the lefthand toolbar, select Settings > General .

On the lefthand toolbar, select Settings > General .

All components require a project description. Write in your project description section, “This component is an example component, and is meant for demonstration purposes only.”

All components require a project description. Write in your project description section, “This component is an example component, and is meant for demonstration purposes only.”

Click Save changes to save your description.

Click Save changes to save your description.

Expand Visibility, project features, permissions .

Expand Visibility, project features, permissions .

Turn on the CI/CD Catalog project toggle. Click Save changes . This will now make the project a CI/CD Catalog project. Any templates in the templates directory will now be available to any project, provided we make a release. Next we will use a component to make a release on our new custom component.

Turn on the CI/CD Catalog project toggle. Click Save changes .

headings: GitLab CI/CD - Hands-On Lab: Working with CI/CD Components
text:
ck Save changes . This will now make the project a CI/CD Catalog project. Any templates in the templates directory will now be available to any project, provided we make a release. Next we will use a component to make a release on our new custom component.

Turn on the CI/CD Catalog project toggle. Click Save changes .

This will now make the project a CI/CD Catalog project. Any templates in the templates directory will now be available to any project, provided we make a release. Next we will use a component to make a release on our new custom component.

In the repository of your Example Component project, click the + button, then click the New File option.

In the repository of your Example Component project, click the + button, then click the New File option.

Title the file .gitlab-ci.yml .

Title the file .gitlab-ci.yml .

In the .gitlab-ci.yml file, add the following code snippet. stages : - release release component : stage : release image : registry.gitlab.com/gitlab-org/release-cli:latest script : - echo "Releasing the latest version of our component." release : tag_name : '1.0.$CI_PIPELINE_IID' description : 'The latest component release.' rules : - if : $CI_COMMIT_REF_NAME == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH This code looks similar to our release job we made in a similar lab, but there is one key difference - component releases require a release format in semantic versioning (Major.Minor.Patch). We use the Patch version to differentiate between each commit.

In the .gitlab-ci.yml file, add the following code snippet.

Wait for the pipeline to complete.

Wait for the pipeline to complete.

To verify that the release has successfully completed, navigate to Deploy > Releases to see the released version of your component.

To verify that the release has successfully completed, navigate to Deploy > Releases to see the released version of your component.

Task C. Adding the Created Component to our Project

Navigate to your CI/CD Catalog by clicking on the search bar, and then clicking the Explore option.

Navigate to your CI/CD Catalog by clicking on the search bar, and then clicking the Explore option.

Click on the CI/CD Catalog option on the left.

Click on the CI/CD Catalog option on the left.

Click on the Example component component. This component will be only visible to you.

Click on the Example component component. This component will be only visible to you.

Copy the include statement in the component. It should look similar to this: include : - component : $CI_SERVER_FQDN/training-users/session-0a9ee9b9/iuhrhhmd/example-component/sample-template@v0.4.0

Copy the include statement in the component. It should look similar to this:

Navigate to your CI/CD project by clicking on the Tanuki logo in the top left corner of the page, then click on your project name.

Navigate to your CI/CD project by clicking on the Tanuki logo in the top left corner of the page, then click on your project name.

Select Build > Pipeline Editor .

Select Build > Pipeline Editor .

At the top of your file, below the image, add in the custom component we created earlier. The top of the .gitlab-ci.yml file should look like this: workflow : rules : - if : $CI_COMMIT_TAG when : never - when : always default : image : golang include : - component : $CI_SERVER_FQDN/training-users/session-0a9ee9b9/iuhrhhmd/example-component/sample-template@v0.4.0

At the top of your file, below the image, add in the custom component we created earlier.

The top of the .gitlab-ci.yml file should look like this:

Select Commit changes .

Select Commit changes .

After committing your changes, navigate to the pipeline created for your commit. You will now see a new job named component-job . This job is the custom job we have imported using the include keyword.

After committing your changes, navigate to the pipeline created for your commit. You will now see a new job named component-job . This job is the custom job we have imported using the include keyword.

Let’s try overriding the stage to instead run in the build stage by adding the following to the .gitlab-ci.yml file: include : - component : $CI_SERVER_FQDN/training-users/session-0a9ee9b9/iu6t0rjr/example-component/sample-template@v0.36.0 inputs : stage : build

Let’s try overriding the stage to instead run in the build stage by adding the following to the .gitlab-ci.yml file:

Due to you implementing CI/CD components, Tanuki Enterprises transformed their pipeline infrastructure from fragmented to standardized. Your team can now create a central component catalog where reusable pipeline configurations are published, versioned, and shared across all projects. When a security update or best practice change is needed, they now update a single component and bump the version—projects automatically receive the improvements by updating their component reference. The inputs feature allows your team to customize components for their specific needs while maintaining the core standardized logic, striking the perfect balance between flexibility and consistency. This approach reduced pipeline configuration time for new projects by 80%, ensured consistent security and quality standards across all repositories, and freed your team to focus on building features rather than maintaining CI/CD boilerplate.

You have completed this lab exercise. You can view the other lab guides for this course .

If you wish to make a change to the lab, please submit your changes via Merge Request.
